comments.text: Bombi clutch assembly - apparently late 1960’s and early 1970’s Bombis had a wide range of different clutch assemblies. This might sound like bad news, but I think it might actually make things easier, because it all depends on the engine put into the Bombi frame, and apparently Bombardier simply used standard North American truck engines. So your first task is to determine if you have a GM, Ford, or Chrysler engine; and which configuration it is (apparently GM inline 4 and 6 cylinder are among the most common). From that point it should be straight forward (note I didn’t say simple), but apparently you can get parts and repair kits from NAPA, Canadian Tire, and Part Source. Good luck!

Fighting mud is no fun. Better tires will help. The Cordtage would need so much work to bring it up to code and living condition.  Glamping in it to build a proper house then renovation is the better option.
  Suggestions for you, get your foundation, drainage, and floor framed in. Get an in progress inspection for the inspector to sign off on that completed part. Next shift to doing the ground work and getting the site smoothed back out. Having an even ground around the house makes the rest of the building so much easier and safer. They little time you lose waiting for the inspection is worth the speed you gain for the rest of the building.

A couple of thoughts.

Orient the house so the front faces west.  This will give you the best sunsets.  And put plenty of windows on the side that faces south.

Don't put the hives in the field with the flowers; you dont want the bees where they will interfere with people and they will find the flowers.  I would put them on the edge of your property and maybe a second or third hive on the opposite edge.

comments.text: Municipalities think they own your property.  Way too many rules, often about things they don't understand or don't matter. BTW 3 metres around the footprint is absolute minimum. Also that is the minimum for fire safety.  Go bigger if you can. You want small plantings near the house not huge trees. Get some antivibration gloves, you will thank me in a couple of years, also a dispenser for marking paint spray cans.  If you are really doing this yourself you will need a transit for layout. The rentals are ridiculous so try and buy a used one.  Brushog for the trails. No question. For both the dump truck and the dozer, they usually have 2 batteries. Maybe google the models and see for sure. BTW you can fill those splits in the steering wheel with epoxy or put a cover on it.  Sooner or later they will bite you.

All homes should be built to accommodate the elderly and disabled. This will be better for everyone.
Large wide doorways and hallways make using a cane, walker or wheel chair much easier.
Bathrooms or wet rooms need to have flat floors and no shower hump or pan. A flat floor allows for easy access and drains need to provided.
Easy to use lever door and faucet handles are easier for the elderly to grip and open.
Main floors should include a master bedroom, restroom, shower, laundry and wide walk in closet with few steps to enter.
Homes should be designed with aging in place in mind.
